scenario: A first-year medical technology student is studying microbiology. She reads that viruses cannot be grown on blood agar or nutrient broth, unlike bacteria.
question: Which fundamental property of viruses BEST explains why they cannot be cultured on artificial culture media?

options.A: Viruses are too small to be seen with the naked eye
options.B: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites dependent on host cell enzymes to reproduce
options.C: Viruses contain only one kind of nucleic acid
options.D: Viruses are filterable agents smaller than bacteria

rationale: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites. They lack the metabolic machinery needed for independent replication and depend entirely on host cell enzymes and organelles. This is why they can only be grown in living systems: tissue culture, embryonated eggs, or live animals.

scenario: During a virology lecture, the professor asks students to identify which virus represents the exception to the general rule about nucleic acid in viruses.
question: A student correctly states that \'DNA is mostly double-stranded EXCEPT\...\' --- which virus completes this statement?

options.A: Reoviridae
options.B: Picornavirus
options.C: Parvovirus
options.D: Poxvirus

rationale: Parvovirus is the exception among DNA viruses --- it contains single-stranded DNA (ssDNA). The rule is: RNA is mostly single-stranded EXCEPT Reoviridae (dsRNA), and DNA is mostly double-stranded EXCEPT Parvovirus (ssDNA).

scenario: A virology exam question asks students to categorize viruses by their capsid symmetry. The student must identify which virus family uses COMPLEX symmetry.
question: Which of the following viruses is correctly classified as having COMPLEX symmetry, making it an exception to the standard icosahedral and helical forms?

options.A: Poliovirus
options.B: Influenza virus
options.C: Poxvirus
options.D: Herpesvirus

rationale: Poxvirus has complex symmetry --- it is the only major human virus with this structural form. It is also the largest of the DNA viruses. Poliovirus is naked icosahedral; influenza is enveloped helical; herpesvirus is enveloped icosahedral.

scenario: A researcher wants to visualize a virus she suspects is the largest known animal virus. She considers which microscope technique would be appropriate.
question: Which statement is TRUE regarding the size and visualization of Poxvirus?

options.A: Poxvirus is 22--26 nm and requires electron microscopy
options.B: Poxvirus is 200--450 nm and can be seen with a light microscope
options.C: Poxvirus is the smallest DNA virus and is best seen with negative staining
options.D: Poxvirus is similar in size to Parvovirus and requires electron microscopy

rationale: Poxvirus, measuring 200--450 nm in length, is the LARGEST animal virus and is unique in that its virions can actually be visualized using a light microscope, unlike most other viruses that require electron microscopy.

scenario: During a lecture on viral replication, a professor describes a stage where infectious virus particles CANNOT be recovered from infected cells even though the cell is alive.
question: Which stage of viral replication is being described?

options.A: Adsorption phase
options.B: Assembly phase
options.C: Eclipse phase (Uncoating)
options.D: Release phase

rationale: The Eclipse phase begins when the nucleic acid is uncoated --- at this point, no intact infectious virions can be recovered from the cell because viral components are being synthesized separately. This phase lasts until new virions are assembled.

scenario: A medical student is reviewing how enveloped viruses differ from non-enveloped viruses in terms of susceptibility to chemical disinfectants.
question: A nurse uses alcohol-based hand rub during a viral outbreak. Which type of virus would be MOST susceptible to this intervention?

options.A: Naked icosahedral viruses like Poliovirus
options.B: Enveloped viruses because the envelope is damaged by alcohols and detergents
options.C: Both enveloped and non-enveloped viruses equally
options.D: Non-enveloped viruses because they lack a protective outer layer

rationale: Enveloped viruses are more susceptible to inactivation by alcohols, detergents, heat, and extreme pH because these agents damage the lipid envelope. Non-enveloped (naked) viruses are more resistant to these environmental factors.

scenario: A patient develops an infection from a virus transmitted via the fecal-oral route. The student is asked to identify the first step that allows the virus to enter host cells.
question: What is the FIRST step in viral infection of a host cell?

options.A: Penetration via pinocytosis (viropexis)
options.B: Adsorption --- attachment to specific receptors on the cell surface
options.C: Uncoating of the nucleic acid
options.D: Assembly of new viral particles

rationale: Adsorption is the FIRST step in viral replication. The viral attachment protein recognizes and binds to specific receptors (protein, carbohydrate, or lipid) on the host cell surface. Cells lacking appropriate receptors are not susceptible to infection.

scenario: A child is brought to the clinic with a characteristic rash. The physician orders a tissue culture. Under microscopy, the pathologist notes morphological changes in infected cells.
question: What is the term for ANY detectable morphological change in host cells resulting from viral infection?

options.A: Viral tropism
options.B: Cytopathic effect (CPE)
options.C: Eclipse phase
options.D: Latent infection

rationale: Cytopathic effect (CPE) refers to any detectable morphological change in host cells caused by viral infection. This includes cell rounding, disorientation, swelling or shrinking, cell death, and detachment from surfaces. CPE is a key tool in laboratory viral diagnosis.

scenario: A healthcare worker accidentally sustains a needlestick injury. The infection control team discusses transmission routes for the causative virus.
question: Which mode of viral transmission is described when a virus is spread from mother to offspring during pregnancy or at delivery?

options.A: Horizontal transmission
options.B: Aerogenic transmission
options.C: Vertical transmission
options.D: Fomite transmission

rationale: Vertical transmission refers to spread from mother to offspring --- either transplacentally (in utero), during delivery (perinatally), or via breast milk. Horizontal transmission refers to spread within a group (person-to-person). When offspring are born infected, the term \'connatal infection\' is used.

scenario: During a board exam review, a student must correctly match virus families with their nucleocapsid characteristics.
question: A student correctly lists the \'rules\' of DNA virus classification. Which of the following is the CORRECT complete statement?

options.A: All DNA viruses are icosahedral EXCEPT Poxvirus (complex); all multiply in the nucleus EXCEPT Poxvirus
options.B: All DNA viruses are enveloped EXCEPT Parvovirus; all are double-stranded EXCEPT Adenovirus
options.C: All DNA viruses multiply in the cytoplasm EXCEPT Poxvirus; all are double-stranded
options.D: All DNA viruses are naked EXCEPT Herpesviridae; all are icosahedral EXCEPT Hepadnaviridae

rationale: The key rules for DNA viruses: (1) All are double-stranded DNA EXCEPT Parvovirus (ssDNA); (2) All are icosahedral EXCEPT Poxvirus (complex symmetry); (3) All are naked EXCEPT Poxvirus and Herpesviridae (enveloped); (4) All multiply in the NUCLEUS EXCEPT Poxvirus (cytoplasm).

scenario: A student reviews the taxonomy of viruses. She must understand how viruses are named and classified at each taxonomic level.
question: Which suffix is CORRECTLY matched to its taxonomic level in virus classification?

options.A: -viridae for genus; -virus for family
options.B: -viridae for family; -virus for genus
options.C: -viridae for species; -virus for order
options.D: -viridae for order; -virus for subfamily

rationale: In virus taxonomy: Family names end in \'-viridae\' (e.g., Herpesviridae, Poxviridae), and genus names carry the suffix \'-virus\' (e.g., Orthopoxvirus, Simplexvirus). Species are then named within each genus.

options.A: Cell lysis, releasing all virions at once
options.B: Budding from the cellular membrane during the late stage of replication
options.C: Exocytosis via the Golgi apparatus without membrane acquisition
options.D: Secretion through viral-encoded pores in the host membrane

rationale: Enveloped viruses acquire their lipid envelope from the host cell membrane during the process of budding in the late stage of replication. This allows enveloped viruses to exit without necessarily killing the cell, contributing to persistent infections. In contrast, non-enveloped viruses typically exit by cell lysis.

scenario: A public health officer reviews which viruses can be grown in which laboratory systems. They need to identify appropriate growth media for studying different viruses.
question: Which of the following laboratory growth systems is APPROPRIATE for cultivating viruses?

options.A: Blood agar plates
options.B: MacConkey agar
options.C: Chick embryo, tissue culture, or animal inoculation
options.D: Nutrient broth with glucose supplement

rationale: Viruses cannot grow on artificial culture media. They require living host cells. The three main laboratory cultivation systems are: (1) Tissue culture (cell lines), (2) Embryonated chick eggs (chorioallantoic membrane), and (3) Live animal inoculation.

scenario: An instructor asks students to identify which viral structural component stimulates antibody (Ab) production and serves as the primary immune target.
question: Which component of the virus is responsible for BOTH protecting the nucleic acid AND stimulating Antigen-Antibody (Ag-Ab) production?

options.A: The viral envelope glycoproteins
options.B: The nucleic acid core (DNA or RNA)
options.C: The viral capsid (made of capsomeres)
options.D: The matrix protein

rationale: The viral capsid (composed of capsomeres made up of protomeres/polypeptides) serves two functions: (1) protecting the nucleic acid core from environmental damage, and (2) stimulating antigen-antibody production (immunogenicity). The envelope, when present, also carries antigenic glycoproteins.

scenario: During a lecture on viral pathogenesis, a professor distinguishes between different outcomes of viral infection at the cellular level.
question: A virus infects a liver cell and inserts its genome into the host chromosome without causing immediate cell destruction or active replication. This outcome is BEST described as:

options.A: Cytocidal infection
options.B: Tumor transformation
options.C: Latent infection
options.D: Noncytocidal infection

rationale: Latent infection occurs when the viral genome is present inside the cell (often integrated), resulting in NEITHER active viral replication NOR cell destruction. The virus remains dormant and can reactivate later. Classic examples include Herpes simplex and Varicella-zoster viruses.

options.A: Specific humoral immune response (antibody-mediated)
options.B: Specific cellular immune response (T-cell mediated)
options.C: Non-specific immune response
options.D: Complement-mediated immune response

rationale: Interferons belong to the NON-SPECIFIC immune response. They are produced early in infection (before adaptive immunity develops) and serve to alert neighboring cells, inhibit viral replication, activate NK cells, and enhance the overall immune response.

options.A: Picornavirus
options.B: Reoviridae
options.C: Togavirus
options.D: Parvovirus

rationale: Reoviridae is the exception among RNA viruses --- it has double-stranded RNA (dsRNA). All other major RNA viruses (Picornaviridae, Togaviridae, Paramyxoviridae, Orthomyxoviridae, etc.) have single-stranded RNA. Remember: ssDNA exception = Parvovirus; dsRNA exception = Reoviridae.

scenario: A student needs to memorize the five basic structural forms of viruses for the board exam.
question: Which of the following is an example of an ENVELOPED HELICAL virus?

options.A: Poliovirus (Naked icosahedral)
options.B: Herpesvirus (Enveloped icosahedral)
options.C: Rabies virus (Enveloped helical)
options.D: Adenovirus (Naked icosahedral)

rationale: Rabies virus, along with influenza, parainfluenza, mumps, and measles viruses, are classified as ENVELOPED HELICAL viruses. Herpesviruses (enveloped icosahedral) and poliovirus/adenovirus (naked icosahedral) are separate categories. Poxvirus alone has complex symmetry.

scenario: A medical technology licensure exam question asks about the critical property of viral attachment that determines host and tissue specificity.
question: What determines whether a particular cell is susceptible to a given virus?

options.A: The size of the virus relative to the cell
options.B: The presence of appropriate receptors on the cell surface recognized by the viral attachment protein
options.C: The metabolic activity of the target cell
options.D: The pH of the extracellular environment

rationale: Viral tropism (tissue specificity) is determined by the interaction between the viral attachment protein and specific receptors on the host cell surface. Cells without appropriate receptors (proteins, carbohydrates, or lipids) are not susceptible to infection. This is why different viruses infect different organs.

scenario: A researcher is working with a virus suspected to be the SMALLEST known DNA virus of animals.
question: Which virus is correctly identified as the smallest DNA virus, and what is its approximate size?

options.A: Poxvirus --- 200 to 450 nm
options.B: Herpesvirus --- 120 to 300 nm
options.C: Parvovirus --- 22 to 26 nm
options.D: Adenovirus --- 70 to 90 nm

rationale: Parvovirus (from Latin \'parvus\' = small) is the SMALLEST DNA virus of animals, measuring only 22--26 nm in diameter. In contrast, Poxvirus is the LARGEST at 200--450 nm. Picornavirus (from Italian \'piccolo\' = small) is the smallest RNA virus.

options.A: Respiratory route
options.B: Fecal-oral (alimentary) route
options.C: Injection/parenteral route
options.D: Arthropod bite

rationale: Hepatitis A virus (HAV) enters through the alimentary/fecal-oral route --- indirect transmission. In contrast, Hepatitis B virus is transmitted parenterally (injection/bloodborne). This distinction is clinically important for infection control measures.

scenario: A student must recall which vaccine type is used for different viral infections, noting that some vaccines use live organisms while others use killed preparations.
question: The smallpox vaccine (Vaccinia) is classified as which type of vaccine?

options.A: Killed (inactivated) vaccine
options.B: Subunit (recombinant) vaccine
options.C: Live virus vaccine
options.D: Toxoid vaccine

rationale: The smallpox vaccine (Vaccinia) is a LIVE virus vaccine --- making it a \'Jennerian vaccine\' named after Edward Jenner (1796). It uses the live vaccinia virus (not variola) to confer cross-protection against smallpox. This is one of the first examples of a successful vaccination program in history.

options.A: Influenza virus
options.B: Adenovirus
options.C: Rotavirus
options.D: Parvovirus B19

rationale: Adenovirus produces a characteristic CPE of \'rounding and clustering of cells with refractile intranuclear inclusion bodies\' --- often described as a \'grape-like cluster\' cytopathic effect. This is a key laboratory identification feature of adenovirus in cell culture.

scenario: A nurse preparing chemoprophylaxis asks the pharmacist what the difference is between chemoprophylaxis and post-exposure prophylaxis in virology.
question: Which BEST defines chemoprophylaxis in the context of viral infection prevention?

options.A: Using antibiotics to prevent secondary bacterial infections after viral illness
options.B: Administering a chemotherapeutic agent when infection is EXPECTED, before diagnosis
options.C: Giving antiviral drugs to treat an established viral infection
options.D: Administering a vaccine after exposure to prevent disease

rationale: Chemoprophylaxis involves administering a chemotherapeutic (antiviral) agent when infection is EXPECTED (anticipated exposure) rather than AFTER it has been diagnosed. This is different from post-exposure prophylaxis (given after a known exposure) and treatment (given after confirmed infection).

scenario: A board exam question asks about which antiviral agent works by inhibiting REVERSE TRANSCRIPTASE, a key enzyme in HIV replication.
question: Which antiviral drug class targets reverse transcriptase and is used against HIV?

options.A: Acyclovir and Famciclovir
options.B: Amantadine and Rimantadine
options.C: AZT (Zidovudine), ddI, and 3TC (Lamivudine)
options.D: Nelfinavir and Ritonavir

rationale: AZT (Zidovudine), ddI, ddC, d4T, 3TC (Lamivudine), and FTC are all REVERSE TRANSCRIPTASE INHIBITORS used against HIV. Acyclovir and Famciclovir inhibit DNA polymerase (used for HSV/VZV). Amantadine/Rimantadine inhibit uncoating (used for Influenza A). Nelfinavir/Ritonavir are HIV protease inhibitors.

scenario: A virology professor challenges students to identify which virus is incorrectly matched with its structural category based on the five basic structural forms.
question: Which of the following virus-to-structural-category pairings is INCORRECT?

options.A: Hepatitis A virus --- Naked icosahedral
options.B: Mumps virus --- Enveloped helical
options.C: Tobacco mosaic virus --- Naked helical
options.D: Yellow fever virus --- Enveloped helical

rationale: Yellow fever virus is an ENVELOPED ICOSAHEDRAL virus (Flaviviridae), NOT enveloped helical. Enveloped helical viruses include rabies, influenza, parainfluenza, mumps, and measles. Rubella virus is another example of an enveloped icosahedral virus. ----------------------------------------------------------------------- **TOPIC 2** *DNA Viruses Part 1: Parvoviridae · Papillomaviridae · Polyomaviridae · Adenoviridae* -----------------------------------------------------------------------

scenario: A 7-year-old child presents to the pediatric clinic with a \'slapped-cheek\' appearance --- bright red redness on both cheeks --- along with a mild fever and lacy rash on the body. The child had been well previously and attends school regularly.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY causative agent of this presentation?

options.A: Human Herpesvirus 6 (Roseola infantum)
options.B: Parvovirus B19 (Erythema infectiosum / Fifth Disease)
options.C: Rubella virus
options.D: Coxsackievirus A16 (Hand-foot-mouth disease)

rationale: The \'slapped-cheek\' appearance with subsequent lacy rash is the hallmark of Erythema infectiosum (Fifth Disease) caused by Parvovirus B19. This is a biphasic illness: the first phase includes viral symptoms, and the second phase (the rash) appears as the immune response develops. The child is no longer infectious once the rash appears.

scenario: A 28-year-old woman who is 18 weeks pregnant attends a prenatal visit. Her 5-year-old at home was recently diagnosed with Fifth Disease (Erythema infectiosum). She is concerned about risks to her unborn child.
question: What is the MOST SERIOUS potential complication of Parvovirus B19 infection during pregnancy?

options.A: Congenital rubella syndrome with deafness and cataracts
options.B: Hydrops fetalis due to fetal anemia and aplastic crisis
options.C: Neonatal herpes with encephalitis
options.D: Cytomegalovirus-related microcephaly

rationale: Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid precursor cells and causes transient aplastic crisis. In the fetus, this leads to severe anemia, high-output heart failure, and accumulation of fluid --- resulting in Hydrops fetalis. This affects 2--3% of infected pregnant women and is the most severe fetal complication. Management includes intrauterine blood transfusion.

scenario: A 35-year-old sickle cell disease patient presents with severe anemia and fatigue. He appears pale with no rash. CBC shows severely low reticulocyte count. His hemoglobin dropped from a baseline of 9 g/dL to 4 g/dL.
question: Which virus is MOST LIKELY responsible for this acute exacerbation in this sickle cell patient?

options.A: Epstein-Barr Virus
options.B: Human Herpesvirus 8
options.C: Parvovirus B19
options.D: Cytomegalovirus

rationale: Parvovirus B19 replicates in bone marrow erythrocyte precursor cells, destroying them and causing a Transient Aplastic Crisis. In patients with underlying hemolytic anemias (sickle cell disease, thalassemia), who rely on high erythropoietic activity, this transient suppression can be LIFE-THREATENING, causing rapid hemoglobin drops.

options.A: Herpes Simplex Virus type 2
options.B: Human Papillomavirus (HPV)
options.C: Molluscum contagiosum virus
options.D: Cytomegalovirus

rationale: Koilocytes are squamous cells with perinuclear halos and nuclear irregularities (nuclear atypia) caused by HPV replication in the cytoplasm. They are the hallmark histological finding of HPV infection and are visible on Pap smear. This is a key laboratory diagnosis tool since HPV is non-culturable.

options.A: 30% of cervical cancers; E1 and E2 proteins inhibit host cell apoptosis
options.B: 70% of cervical cancers; E6 and E7 proteins interact with cellular tumor suppressor proteins
options.C: 50% of cervical cancers; E4 and E5 proteins disrupt the cell cycle
options.D: 90% of cervical cancers; L1 and L2 proteins form the viral capsid and transform cells

rationale: HPV genotypes 16 and 18 cause approximately 70% of all cervical cancers. They produce oncoproteins E6 (which degrades p53 tumor suppressor) and E7 (which inactivates Rb protein), driving uncontrolled cell proliferation and malignant transformation. High-risk genotypes include 16, 18, 31, 33, and others.

scenario: A 4-year-old child is brought with hoarseness and a \'barking cough.\' Laryngoscopy reveals multiple small wart-like growths in the larynx. The mother recalls having genital warts during delivery.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis and route of transmission?

options.A: Epiglottitis caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b
options.B: Laryngeal papillomatosis caused by HPV, transmitted vertically during delivery
options.C: Croup caused by Parainfluenza virus, transmitted via respiratory droplets
options.D: Laryngeal carcinoma caused by HPV genotypes 16/18, transmitted sexually

rationale: Laryngeal papillomatosis (recurrent respiratory papillomatosis) in children is caused by HPV (typically genotypes 6 and 11) transmitted vertically during vaginal delivery from a mother with genital warts. This is a route of spread: vertical transmission during delivery --- hence C-section may be considered in mothers with active genital warts.

scenario: A college health center nurse is counseling students about HPV prevention. She explains that the HPV vaccine should ideally be administered before sexual activity.
question: Which HPV genotypes are covered by the GARDASIL vaccine (quadrivalent), and what is the recommended age for vaccination?

options.A: HPV 6, 11, 16, 18 --- recommended at 9 to 26 years old
options.B: HPV 16 and 18 only --- recommended at 25 to 45 years old
options.C: HPV 31, 33, 45 --- recommended after first sexual encounter
options.D: HPV 6 and 11 only --- recommended at 12 to 15 years old

rationale: Gardasil (quadrivalent HPV vaccine) covers genotypes 6, 11 (low-risk, cause genital warts) and 16, 18 (high-risk, cause most cervical cancers). Cervarix covers genotypes 16 and 18 only. It is recommended that the vaccine be administered at a young age (9--26 years), ideally before sexual activity, as it is most effective as primary prevention.

scenario: A renal transplant patient on immunosuppressive therapy develops hematuria, dysuria, and urinary frequency. Urinalysis shows decoy cells. PCR of urine is positive for a polyomavirus.
question: Which polyomavirus and associated condition is MOST LIKELY responsible for this presentation?

options.A: JC virus (Polyomavirus hominis 2) causing Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy
options.B: BK virus (Polyomavirus hominis 1) causing hemorrhagic cystitis and urethral strictures
options.C: Merkel cell polyomavirus causing cutaneous malignancy
options.D: HPV type 6 causing condyloma acuminatum

rationale: BK virus (Polyomavirus hominis 1) reactivates in immunosuppressed patients --- particularly renal transplant patients --- and causes hemorrhagic cystitis, urethral strictures, and nephropathy. Treatment involves reducing immunosuppression and possibly cidofovir. PCR is the diagnostic method of choice.

scenario: An AIDS patient with CD4 count of 50 cells/μL develops progressive neurological symptoms including cognitive decline, weakness, and sensory loss. MRI shows multiple white matter lesions.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY etiology, and which virus causes this condition?

options.A: Toxoplasma gondii causing toxoplasmosis (ring-enhancing lesions)
options.B: JC virus (Polyomavirus hominis 2) causing Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML)
options.C: Cryptococcus neoformans causing cryptococcal meningitis
options.D: CMV causing CMV encephalitis

rationale: PML (Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy) is caused by reactivation of JC virus (John Cunningham virus / Polyomavirus hominis 2) in severely immunosuppressed patients (CD4 \< 200). Prior to antiretroviral therapy, PML developed in 2--4% of AIDS patients. It causes demyelination of white matter with rapid progression --- often fatal within 1 year. There is no effective treatment for PML.

scenario: Military recruits at a training camp experience an outbreak of acute respiratory illness. The outbreak affects primarily young adults who have not been previously vaccinated.
question: Which adenovirus serotypes are primarily associated with respiratory illness in ADULTS (military recruits), and what vaccine is available?

options.A: Ad 40 and 41 --- vaccine available for civilians
options.B: Ad 2 and 5 --- no vaccine available
options.C: Ad 3 and 7 --- live oral vaccine approved for military personnel aged 17--50
options.D: Ad 8 and 19 --- vaccine prevents viral conjunctivitis

rationale: Adenovirus serotypes 3 and 7 (and also serotype 4) cause significant respiratory illness in adults, particularly military recruits. A live oral adenovirus vaccine containing serotypes 4 and 7 is approved for military personnel between ages 17 and 50. Serotypes 2 and 5 are more common in children. Serotypes 40 and 41 cause enteric disease.

scenario: A 10-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with painful urination and pink urine (hematuria). He has no fever, no back pain, and urine culture is negative. He has no history of UTI.
question: Which adenovirus serotype is MOST LIKELY responsible for this presentation and which age group is primarily affected?

options.A: Ad 40 and 41 --- affects children under 2 years (gastroenteritis)
options.B: Ad 8 and 19 --- affects adults (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis)
options.C: Ad 11 and 21 --- affects boys aged 6--15 (acute hemorrhagic cystitis)
options.D: Ad 3 and 7 --- affects military recruits (respiratory disease)

rationale: Acute Hemorrhagic Cystitis (AHC) in children is primarily associated with Adenovirus serotypes 11 and 21. It mainly affects boys aged 6--15 years and presents with sudden-onset gross hematuria (pink/red urine) and dysuria, without evidence of bacterial infection. This is a self-limiting condition.

scenario: Several children from the same swim team present to a clinic with red eyes (conjunctivitis), sore throat, and high fever after attending a swim camp. The pool water is reported to be poorly chlorinated.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis, and which adenovirus serotypes are responsible?

options.A: Viral keratoconjunctivitis caused by Ad 8 and 19
options.B: Pharyngoconjunctival fever caused by Ad 3 and 7
options.C: Acute follicular conjunctivitis associated with swimming pools caused by Ad 40/41
options.D: Pink Eye (epidemic conjunctivitis) caused by Ad 8 and 19, transmitted via contaminated pool water

rationale: Pharyngoconjunctival Fever (PCF) is characterized by the triad: PHARYNGITIS + CONJUNCTIVITIS + SPIKING FEVER. It is frequently associated with inadequately chlorinated swimming pools and is primarily caused by Adenovirus serotypes 3 and 7. Adequate chlorination is usually sufficient to prevent outbreaks.

scenario: In a clinical virology laboratory, a technician is processing a stool specimen from a 2-year-old child with diarrhea, fever, and vomiting. The physician suspects adenoviral gastroenteritis.
question: Which adenovirus serotypes cause viral gastroenteritis and what is notable about them in cell culture?

options.A: Ad 3 and 7 --- grow readily in standard cell cultures
options.B: Ad 8 and 19 --- cause epidemic diarrhea in schools
options.C: Ad 40 and 41 --- fastidious; cannot grow in standard cell culture (enteric adenoviruses)
options.D: Ad 11 and 21 --- cause diarrhea and hematuria in infants

rationale: Adenovirus serotypes 40 and 41 are the ENTERIC ADENOVIRUSES causing viral gastroenteritis in children under 2 years (diarrhea, fever, vomiting). They are \'fastidious\' --- they CANNOT be grown in standard cell culture systems, which is an important laboratory distinction. Special cell lines are required for their growth.

scenario: A laboratory receives a respiratory specimen from a hospitalized child with suspected adenoviral pneumonia. The virology technician sets up cell culture.
question: What is the characteristic Cytopathic Effect (CPE) of adenovirus in cell culture?

options.A: Syncytia (multinucleated giant cells) formation
options.B: Focal areas of cell lysis with plaque formation
options.C: Grape-like cluster CPE with rounding and clustering of cells with refractile intranuclear inclusion bodies
options.D: Intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies (Guarnieri bodies)

rationale: Adenovirus produces a characteristic \'grape-like cluster\' CPE: rounding and clustering of cells with refractile intranuclear inclusion bodies. Growth occurs in 2--5 days after inoculation. Cell lines used include A-549, HEp-2, and HeLa cells. Confirmatory testing uses indirect fluorescent antibody (IFA) or enzyme immunoassay (EIA).

options.A: It causes transient aplastic crisis that resolves without treatment
options.B: It causes persistent chronic B19 infection due to poor immune clearance, leading to chronic anemia
options.C: It causes hydrops fetalis in male recipients
options.D: It exclusively causes Fifth Disease regardless of immune status

rationale: In immunocompromised patients (HIV/AIDS, transplant recipients, congenital immunodeficiency), Parvovirus B19 cannot be cleared effectively. This leads to PERSISTENT CHRONIC B19 INFECTION with ongoing destruction of erythroid precursors and chronic anemia (pure red cell aplasia). Treatment includes intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG).

scenario: A dermatologist is counseling a patient newly diagnosed with Epidermodysplasia verruciformis. The patient has a history of HIV/AIDS with a CD4 count of 120 cells/μL.
question: Which HPV type is associated with Epidermodysplasia verruciformis, and what is the clinical significance?

options.A: HPV 6 and 11 --- cause benign condylomata acuminata with no malignant potential
options.B: HPV types 5, 8, 9, and 12 --- associated with T-cell deficiency; considered a PRE-MALIGNANT condition
options.C: HPV 16 and 18 --- cause invasive cervical cancer in immunocompetent patients
options.D: HPV 1 --- causes plantar warts with rare malignant transformation

rationale: Epidermodysplasia verruciformis (EV) is characterized by multiple flat wart-like lesions all over the body, associated with T-cell deficiency (HIV/AIDS, congenital immunodeficiency). Specific HPV types (5, 8, 9, etc.) are responsible. Importantly, EV is a PRE-MALIGNANT condition --- lesions can progress to squamous cell carcinoma, especially in sun-exposed areas.

scenario: A couple is told they need to be tested for HPV. The partner (male) wants to know if he can also develop complications from HPV infection.
question: Which cancers in MALES are associated with high-risk HPV infection?

options.A: Prostate cancer and bladder cancer
options.B: Squamous cell carcinoma of the penis, anus, and rectum; some laryngeal carcinomas
options.C: Hepatocellular carcinoma and Kaposi sarcoma
options.D: Testicular cancer and penile melanoma

rationale: In males, high-risk HPV types (16, 18, and others) are associated with squamous cell carcinoma of the PENIS, ANUS, and RECTUM, as well as some LARYNGEAL carcinomas. HPV affects both sexes and all age groups. Men who have sex with men (MSM) are at higher risk for anal HPV infection and anal cancer.

scenario: A physician treats a patient with multiple plantar warts (verruca plantaris). She selects an appropriate topical agent for treatment.
question: Which treatment is CORRECTLY matched to its primary indication for HPV-related lesions?

options.A: Liquid nitrogen (cryotherapy) --- for genital warts/condylomata
options.B: Podophyllin --- for plantar warts
options.C: Salicylic acid --- for plantar warts; Podophyllin --- for genital warts
options.D: Alpha interferon --- for skin warts only, not genital lesions

rationale: Treatment of HPV lesions is site-specific: SALICYLIC ACID is used for plantar warts (verruca plantaris); PODOPHYLLIN (antimitotic agent) is used for genital warts/condylomata; LIQUID NITROGEN (cryotherapy) can be used for skin warts; ALPHA INTERFERON has anti-HPV activity. The vaccine (Gardasil/Cervarix) is for prevention, not treatment.

scenario: A medical laboratory science instructor gives a quiz on the laboratory diagnosis of Parvovirus B19.
question: Which specimen and test combination is MOST APPROPRIATE for diagnosing acute Parvovirus B19 infection?

options.A: Stool specimen --- electron microscopy for viral particles
options.B: Serum --- Parvovirus IgM antibody testing (indicates recent infection)
options.C: Skin biopsy --- histology showing inclusion bodies
options.D: Urine --- PCR for Parvovirus B19 DNA

rationale: For ACUTE Parvovirus B19 infection, SERUM testing for Parvovirus-specific IgM antibody is the primary test --- a positive result indicates RECENT infection. For immunocompromised patients or aplastic crisis (where IgM may be absent), SERUM Parvovirus DNA by PCR is used. Cord blood testing uses both Parvovirus DNA PCR and IgM.

scenario: A student is preparing for boards and reviews the laboratory diagnosis of adenovirus infections.
question: Which statement about adenovirus laboratory diagnosis is CORRECT?

options.A: Adenovirus requires refrigeration during transport due to temperature sensitivity
options.B: Adenovirus is confirmed using Pap smear showing koilocytes
options.C: Adenovirus is stable without refrigeration; CPE shows grape-like clustering; confirmed by IFA or EIA
options.D: Adenovirus is diagnosed by detecting IgM antibodies using ELISA within 24 hours

rationale: Key laboratory facts for Adenovirus: (1) STABLE --- does NOT need refrigeration during transport; (2) CPE: characteristic grape-like cluster with intranuclear inclusion bodies; (3) Confirmation: Indirect Fluorescent Antibody (IFA) or Enzyme Immunoassay (EIA); (4) Growth in cell lines like A-549, HEp-2, HeLa in 2--5 days; (5) Nucleic acid testing is available.

scenario: A family planning clinic counselor educates a teenage patient about the difference between HPV wart types and their infectious periods.
question: Regarding the INFECTIOUS PERIOD of HPV, which statement is CORRECT?

options.A: Patients are infectious only during the incubation period before warts appear
options.B: Lesions when present are highly infectious; asymptomatic viral shedding may also occur from the genital tract
options.C: Patients become non-infectious once treated with podophyllin
options.D: HPV is only transmitted during penetrative sexual intercourse, not through skin contact

rationale: HPV has a complex infectious period: VISIBLE LESIONS are highly infectious, but ASYMPTOMATIC VIRAL SHEDDING can also occur from the genital tract even without visible warts. Skin lesions become non-infectious once they disappear. The incubation period for genital warts is approximately 3 months after exposure.

scenario: A hospital epidemiologist investigates an outbreak of pink eye (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis) among hospital staff in the ophthalmology ward.
question: Which adenovirus serotypes MOST COMMONLY cause epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC / \'Shipyard Eye\'), and what is a key feature of its prevention?

options.A: Serotypes 3 and 7 --- prevented by adequate swimming pool chlorination
options.B: Serotypes 8 and 19 --- highly contagious; prevention requires handwashing, not sharing towels, and isolation
options.C: Serotypes 40 and 41 --- prevented by food handling precautions
options.D: Serotypes 11 and 21 --- prevented by urinary catheter sterilization

rationale: Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis (EKC), also called \'Shipyard Eye\' or \'Pink Eye,\' is caused by Adenovirus serotypes 8 and 19. It is HIGHLY CONTAGIOUS and can spread rapidly in healthcare settings. Prevention requires strict contact precautions: handwashing, not sharing towels or equipment, and isolation (no school/work) during the infectious period of 1--3 weeks.

scenario: A pathology student reviews which viruses cannot be cultured in the virology laboratory using standard techniques.
question: Which of the following viruses is classified as NON-CULTURABLE by standard cell culture methods?

options.A: Adenovirus
options.B: Herpes Simplex Virus
options.C: Human Papillomavirus (HPV)
options.D: Cytomegalovirus

rationale: Human Papillomavirus (HPV) is NON-CULTURABLE in standard laboratory cell culture systems. This is a significant laboratory challenge. Diagnosis relies on clinical examination of lesions, Pap smear (showing koilocytes), and molecular methods such as PCR (most sensitive but not recommended for routine genital wart diagnosis).

scenario: A student is reviewing Human Bocavirus (HBoV) for her virology exam. She recalls key features of this relatively new virus.
question: Which of the following statements about Human Bocavirus (HBoV) is CORRECT?

options.A: HBoV is a DNA parvovirus first described in 2005; most common in summer; causes lower respiratory illness in children aged 7--18 months
options.B: HBoV is an RNA parvovirus first described in 1975; detected by IgM serology
options.C: HBoV is a novel parvovirus described in 2005; most common in WINTER; causes upper and lower RTIs in children 7--18 months; detected by rtPCR
options.D: HBoV is classified in the Polyomaviridae family and causes hemorrhagic cystitis

rationale: Human Bocavirus (HBoV) is a novel parvovirus first described in 2005. It causes upper AND lower respiratory tract infections in children aged 7--18 months. It is most prevalent in WINTER. It is frequently detected as a CO-INFECTION with RSV and hMPV. Detection is best achieved with reverse transcription PCR (rtPCR), reflecting its importance in respiratory illness diagnosis.

scenario: A board-certified specialist in infectious disease is reviewing the treatment options for Polyomavirus hominis 1 (BK virus) infection in an immunocompromised patient.
question: What is the treatment approach and the ONLY antiviral agent that may be effective against BK virus (Polyomavirus hominis 1)?

options.A: Acyclovir --- inhibits DNA polymerase; highly effective
options.B: Oseltamivir --- neuraminidase inhibitor; first-line treatment
options.C: Reducing immunosuppression AND Cidofovir --- the only antiviral with potential efficacy
options.D: Interferon-alpha --- used as monotherapy for complete viral clearance

rationale: Treatment for BK virus (Polyomavirus hominis 1) in transplant patients involves: (1) REDUCING IMMUNOSUPPRESSION (primary strategy) to allow immune reconstitution; and (2) CIDOFOVIR --- the only antiviral agent with potential efficacy, though its use is limited by nephrotoxicity. There is NO effective treatment for JC virus (PML). PCR is used for diagnosis. ----------------------------------------------------------------------- **TOPIC 3** *DNA Viruses: Poxviridae* -----------------------------------------------------------------------

scenario: A 32-year-old public health official is discussing bioterrorism preparedness. She notes that one specific virus is considered a Category A bioterrorism agent and is preserved only in two authorized laboratories worldwide.
question: Which virus is being described, and WHERE are the only two authorized repositories for this virus?

options.A: Monkeypox virus --- stored at WHO Geneva and Beijing, China
options.B: Variola virus (smallpox) --- stored at CDC Atlanta, USA and VECTOR Institute, Moscow, Russia
options.C: Vaccinia virus --- stored at Fort Detrick, USA and Porton Down, UK
options.D: Cowpox virus --- stored at CDC Atlanta and the Pasteur Institute, Paris

rationale: Variola virus (the causative agent of smallpox) is considered a Category A bioterrorism agent. Following eradication in 1980, all remaining stocks were consolidated and are ONLY stored at two WHO-authorized laboratories: the CDC in Atlanta, USA and the VECTOR Institute in Novosibirsk, Russia. This is done to prevent unauthorized access.

scenario: A child presents with a vesicular rash. The physician must differentiate between chickenpox and smallpox. The child has fever before the rash, and lesions are more concentrated on the face and extremities, including palms and soles.
question: Which feature BEST distinguishes SMALLPOX from chickenpox?

options.A: Smallpox: centripetal distribution (trunk \> extremities); chickenpox: centrifugal distribution
options.B: Smallpox: centrifugal distribution, fever PRECEDES rash, all lesions in SAME stage; chickenpox: centripetal, no fever prodrome, lesions in MULTIPLE stages
options.C: Smallpox: superficial lesions; chickenpox: deep lesions with pock mark scarring
options.D: Smallpox: caused by a Parapoxvirus; chickenpox: caused by a Orthopoxvirus

rationale: KEY DIFFERENCES: Smallpox --- CENTRIFUGAL distribution (face, arms, legs including palms/soles); FEVER PRECEDES rash by 2--4 days; all lesions in SAME stage of development (synchronous); lesions are DEEP. Chickenpox --- CENTRIPETAL distribution (trunk \> face/extremities); minimal fever prodrome; lesions in MULTIPLE stages (asynchronous); lesions are SUPERFICIAL.

scenario: A medical student reads about Edward Jenner\'s contribution to vaccinology. She learns that milkmaids who had previously contracted cowpox appeared to be immune to smallpox.
question: What was the HISTORICAL significance of Jenner\'s observation in 1796--1798, and which virus is used in the smallpox vaccine?

options.A: Jenner proved that variola virus could be attenuated; the vaccine uses attenuated variola
options.B: Jenner demonstrated that cowpox infection conferred cross-immunity to smallpox; the vaccine uses VACCINIA virus
options.C: Jenner showed that milkmaids had natural resistance due to genetic factors; the vaccine uses live smallpox virus
options.D: Jenner proved that pasteurization prevents smallpox; the vaccine uses inactivated cowpox virus

rationale: In 1796, Edward Jenner observed that milkmaids infected with cowpox were immune to smallpox and demonstrated the efficacy of vaccination (from \'vacca\' = cow). The modern smallpox vaccine uses VACCINIA VIRUS (not variola or cowpox) --- a Jennerian vaccine named for his work. WHO declared smallpox eradicated in May 1980, with the last natural case in Somalia in 1977.

scenario: A patient presents with hemorrhagic skin lesions and a rapidly progressive systemic illness. They are diagnosed with a rare form of smallpox. The physician notes the prognosis is extremely poor.
question: Which classification of smallpox is associated with almost 100% fatality, characterized by hemorrhagic lesions that are exceedingly difficult to diagnose?

options.A: Classic (Ordinary) discrete subtype --- moderately severe with distinct pustules
options.B: Alastrim (Variola minor) --- milder form with 0.1--2% fatality
options.C: Hemorrhagic smallpox --- almost always fatal within 5--7 days; characterized by severely prostrating prodrome
options.D: Malignant (Flat) smallpox --- confluent lesions that never pustulate; invariably fatal but slower progression

rationale: HEMORRHAGIC SMALLPOX is the most rapidly progressive form, almost always fatal within 5--7 days. It is characterized by a severely prostrating prodromal illness with high fever, headache, back pain, and abdominal pain, followed by hemorrhagic skin lesions. It is difficult to diagnose clinically and exceedingly infectious. Malignant (Flat) smallpox is also invariably fatal but progresses more slowly.

scenario: A veterinarian reports cases of a pox-like illness in cats presenting to her clinic in the UK. Subsequently, several cat owners develop single pustular lesions on their hands.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis in the cat owners, and what is the primary reservoir host for this virus?

options.A: Orf virus infection --- primary reservoir: sheep and goats
options.B: Cowpox virus infection --- primary reservoir: RODENTS (transmitted to cats, then to humans)
options.C: Buffalopox virus infection --- primary reservoir: water buffalo
options.D: Monkeypox virus infection --- primary reservoir: Gambian rats

rationale: Modern COWPOX infections in humans are usually ACQUIRED FROM CATS (not cows). Cats themselves acquire cowpox from RODENTS (the true reservoir host). Humans develop one large pustular skin lesion most commonly on the face or hands, reflecting the contact point with infected cats. The incubation period is 9--10 days. Cowpox can be distinguished from vaccinia by its deep red hemorrhagic lesions on chorioallantoic membrane.

scenario: A 19-year-old male college student presents with multiple small (3--4mm), dome-shaped, flesh-colored papules with a central dimple (umbilication) on his trunk and arms. He reports no pain but finds them cosmetically bothersome.
question: What is the CAUSATIVE AGENT and recommended treatment for this condition?

options.A: Human Papillomavirus (HPV) --- treat with podophyllin
options.B: Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV) --- treat by squeezing lesions, cryotherapy, or diathermy; no antiviral available
options.C: Varicella-zoster virus --- treat with acyclovir
options.D: Herpes Simplex Virus --- treat with valacyclovir

rationale: The description (dome-shaped, umbilicated \[central dimple\] waxy papules) is classic for MOLLUSCUM CONTAGIOSUM (caused by MCV, a Molluscipoxvirus). There is NO antiviral treatment. Options include: squeezing individual lesions to extrude the molluscum body (forcing out infectious virus), cryotherapy (freezing), diathermy (burning), or piercing with podophyllin-dipped instrument. It is self-limiting in immunocompetent individuals.

scenario: An immunocompromised HIV patient with a CD4 count of 50 cells/μL presents with large, unusually extensive molluscum contagiosum lesions on the face, neck, and scalp.
question: What is the SIGNIFICANCE of extensive molluscum contagiosum in an HIV patient?

options.A: It indicates early HIV infection before CD4 decline
options.B: Molluscum contagiosum is seen in late stages of AIDS and can cause severe, extensive disease in immunocompromised patients
options.C: Molluscum contagiosum exclusively affects HIV patients through sexual transmission
options.D: Extensive molluscum in HIV indicates superimposed bacterial skin infection

rationale: Molluscum contagiosum is typically self-limiting in immunocompetent individuals. However, in immunocompromised patients (particularly late-stage AIDS), it can cause a MORE SEVERE FORM of disease with LARGE, EXTENSIVE LESIONS especially on the face, neck, scalp, and upper body --- often resistant to standard treatment. This serves as a clinical marker of severe immune compromise.

scenario: A shepherd from a rural area presents with a single, large (1.5 cm) vesicular lesion on his right index finger that has become pustular and crusted. He handles sheep daily.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis, and how is it definitively diagnosed?

options.A: Anthrax (Bacillus anthracis) cutaneous form --- diagnosed by Gram stain and culture
options.B: Orf virus infection (Parapoxvirus from sheep) --- definitively diagnosed by PCR (only method to definitively identify orf virus)
options.C: Cowpox infection --- diagnosed by chorioallantoic membrane inoculation
options.D: Tularemia (Francisella tularensis) --- diagnosed by serology

rationale: ORF is a Parapoxvirus infection transmitted to humans by DIRECT CONTACT WITH INFECTED SHEEP OR GOATS. It presents as a single large (1--2cm) vesicular-to-pustular lesion on fingers/hands. Key laboratory points: Electron microscopy can CONFIRM Parapoxvirus infection, but only PCR can DEFINITIVELY IDENTIFY the specific parapoxvirus as ORP VIRUS. There is no specific treatment; it resolves in 2--3 weeks.

scenario: A dermatologist examines biopsy specimens from lesions of molluscum contagiosum and sends them for histological examination.
question: What are the characteristic HISTOLOGICAL findings of molluscum contagiosum, and what are the laboratory inclusion bodies called?

options.A: Intranuclear Cowdry type A inclusion bodies --- seen in herpes infections
options.B: Negri bodies --- intracytoplasmic inclusions pathognomonic for rabies
options.C: Molluscum bodies (Henderson-Paterson bodies) --- eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions pushing nucleus aside
options.D: Guarnieri bodies --- cytoplasmic inclusions seen in vaccinia virus infection

rationale: Molluscum contagiosum produces characteristic MOLLUSCUM BODIES (also called Henderson-Paterson bodies) --- large eosinophilic, intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies that push the nucleus to the periphery. These are visible on histological biopsy and are pathognomonic for molluscum contagiosum infection. Laboratory diagnosis is primarily by biopsy and histological examination.

scenario: A health worker preparing for a monkeypox response reads about the different clades and their epidemiological patterns. She needs to advise on the population most at risk during the 2022 global outbreak.
question: Which monkeypox clade caused the 2022 GLOBAL OUTBREAK, and which population was primarily affected?

options.A: Clade Ia (Central Africa) --- primarily affecting children with malnutrition
options.B: Clade Ib (DRC) --- affecting adults via household contact with fatality rate \<0.5%
options.C: Clade IIb (West Africa) --- causing the ongoing global outbreak; primarily affecting adults, especially MSM (men who have sex with men)
options.D: Clade IIa (West Africa) --- insufficient data; affects both children and adults equally

rationale: Clade IIb (West African clade, originally identified in Nigeria) caused the ONGOING GLOBAL MONKEYPOX OUTBREAK beginning in 2022, spreading to more than 100 non-endemic countries. It primarily affected ADULTS, particularly MEN WHO HAVE SEX WITH MEN (MSM). The mortality rate is less than 0.1%. Transmission is mainly via close skin-to-skin contact including sexual contact.

Correct chronology: (1) 1958 --- Monkeypox VIRUS DISCOVERED in laboratory monkey colonies kept for research; (2) 1970 --- FIRST HUMAN CASE recorded in what is now the Democratic Republic of the Congo; (3) 2024 --- LARGE OUTBREAKS in Central and Eastern Africa led to travel-associated cases worldwide. Scientists suspect African rodents and non-human primates as the natural reservoir.

scenario: A travel medicine clinic is preparing a patient for travel to Central Africa. The physician reviews monkeypox prevention strategies.
question: Which vaccine provides protection against monkeypox, and what dosing schedule is currently recommended?

options.A: ACAM2000 --- 2 doses; or JYNNEOS --- 1 dose for all populations
options.B: JYNNEOS --- 2 doses; or ACAM2000 --- 1 dose for specific populations
options.C: Variola vaccine --- 3 doses over 6 months
options.D: No vaccine is effective against monkeypox; only post-exposure cidofovir is used

rationale: Monkeypox vaccine recommendations: JYNNEOS vaccine (modified vaccinia Ankara) --- 2 doses is the primary recommendation. ACAM2000 (live vaccinia) --- 1 dose for specific populations. Importantly, SMALLPOX VACCINATION also confers protection against monkeypox. Prevention also includes avoiding direct contact with infected persons or animals, using PPE, and handwashing.

scenario: A farmer who works with water buffalo in India develops localized pox lesions on his hands. Virological testing confirms Buffalopox virus infection.
question: Which statement CORRECTLY describes Buffalopox virus and its relationship to other poxviruses?

options.A: Buffalopox is a species of Parapoxvirus, distinct from vaccinia, and causes disease only in water buffalo
options.B: Buffalopox is a DERIVATIVE OF VACCINIA VIRUS that has persisted in Indian water buffalo since discontinuation of smallpox vaccination; causes disease indistinguishable from cowpox in animals
options.C: Buffalopox is a zoonosis caused by Orthopoxvirus tauri, primarily seen in cattle worldwide
options.D: Buffalopox is transmitted exclusively by respiratory droplets and cannot be acquired by farmers through skin contact

rationale: Buffalopox virus is a DERIVATIVE OF VACCINIA VIRUS that has persisted in water buffalo in India since smallpox vaccination was discontinued. Disease in buffalo (and occasionally cattle) is indistinguishable from cowpox. Buffalopox CAN BE TRANSMITTED TO HUMANS, causing localized pox lesions. This occurred because vaccinia virus was introduced to buffalo populations during smallpox eradication campaigns.

scenario: A virologist explains why monkeypox can be misidentified as smallpox in clinical presentation but differs in important epidemiological ways.
question: What is the MOST IMPORTANT clinical feature that distinguishes MONKEYPOX from smallpox?

options.A: Monkeypox causes a centripetal rash (trunk predominant) while smallpox is centrifugal
options.B: Monkeypox causes INGUINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY, which is characteristically ABSENT in smallpox
options.C: Monkeypox has a much shorter incubation period (1--3 days) compared to smallpox (10--17 days)
options.D: Monkeypox cannot spread between humans, while smallpox is highly contagious person-to-person

rationale: The KEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURE of monkeypox vs. smallpox is LYMPHADENOPATHY --- specifically INGUINAL (groin) lymphadenopathy. Smallpox does NOT cause significant lymphadenopathy. Both cause similar-appearing rashes (centrifugal, maculo-papulo-vesicular-pustular). Monkeypox also has lower mortality and transmissibility than smallpox. Monkeypox incubation is 3--17 days; smallpox is 10--17 days.

options.A: Orthopoxvirus vaccinia --- transmitted from cows during the vaccination era
options.B: Parapoxvirus (Pseudocowpox virus) --- transmitted from COWS to milkers through direct contact
options.C: Parapoxvirus (Orf virus) --- transmitted from goats during milking
options.D: Molluscipoxvirus --- transmitted from dairy cattle through contaminated equipment

rationale: Milker\'s Node (Pseudocowpox) is caused by a PARAPOXVIRUS (specifically Pseudocowpox virus) transmitted to human milkers through DIRECT CONTACT WITH INFECTED COWS. It presents as single or multiple nodules (1--2 cm vesicular to pustular lesions) on the hands, surrounded by erythema. It resolves in 4--6 weeks without treatment. It CANNOT be passed to other humans.

scenario: A student preparing for boards must memorize key facts about Poxviridae classification and the viruses within each genus.
question: Which pairing of GENUS and VIRUS is CORRECTLY matched within Poxviridae?

options.A: Molluscipoxvirus --- Monkeypox virus
options.B: Yatapoxvirus --- Molluscum contagiosum virus
options.C: Orthopoxvirus --- Variola (major and minor), Vaccinia, Monkeypox, Cowpox
options.D: Parapoxvirus --- Vaccinia and Buffalopox viruses

rationale: ORTHOPOXVIRUS genus includes: Variola major and minor (smallpox), Vaccinia (vaccine strain), Monkeypox, Cowpox, and Buffalopox. PARAPOXVIRUS includes: Orf virus and Pseudocowpox (Milker\'s Node). MOLLUSCIPOXVIRUS: Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV). YATAPOXVIRUS: Tanapoxvirus and Yaba monkey tumor virus. This classification is a common board exam question.

scenario: A laboratory director receives skin lesion material from a patient with suspected smallpox. She selects the appropriate detection methods.
question: Which diagnostic methods are used to detect Poxvirus from skin lesions?

options.A: Pap smear showing koilocytes and PCR
options.B: Electron microscopy of skin lesion material AND PCR
options.C: Shell vial culture with IFA confirmation
options.D: Serology (IgM and IgG) and complement fixation test

rationale: For Poxvirus detection from skin lesions, the recommended methods are: (1) ELECTRON MICROSCOPY of material from skin lesions --- can directly visualize the characteristic large, brick-shaped poxvirus particles; and (2) PCR --- for specific identification of the poxvirus species. The unique large size of poxviruses also allows visualization by LIGHT MICROSCOPY, unlike most other viruses.

scenario: A small-town physician sees a 3-year-old girl with skin lesions that are 2--6 mm, raised, dome-shaped with a central dimple, appearing in clusters on her arms and face. The lesions are flesh-colored and waxy. Mother denies sexual abuse.
question: What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis, and which statement about this infection in CHILDREN is CORRECT?

options.A: Genital warts (HPV) --- commonly sexually transmitted in adults but vertical in children
options.B: Molluscum contagiosum --- COMMON IN CHILDREN via direct contact or fomites (towels, toys, clothing); NON-SEXUAL transmission
options.C: Varicella (chickenpox) --- vesicular rash from HHV-3; treated with acyclovir
options.D: Impetigo --- bacterial infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus; treated with antibiotics

rationale: MOLLUSCUM CONTAGIOSUM is common in children and is transmitted NON-SEXUALLY through direct contact or fomites (contaminated towels, clothing, toys, swimming pools). The classic presentation is small (2--6mm), dome-shaped, umbilicated (central dimple), waxy papules in clusters. In children, this is NOT a marker for sexual abuse. It is a self-limiting disease in immunocompetent individuals.

scenario: During a virology exam review session, the instructor reviews the unique characteristics of Poxvirus replication compared to other DNA viruses.
question: Where does Poxvirus replicate within the host cell, and why is this clinically significant for treatment?

options.A: In the nucleus --- same as all other DNA viruses, using nuclear DNA polymerase
options.B: In the cytoplasm --- Poxvirus is the ONLY major DNA virus that replicates in the CYTOPLASM (not the nucleus), and it encodes its own RNA polymerase
options.C: In the endoplasmic reticulum --- where it acquires its complex envelope
options.D: In mitochondria --- using host mitochondrial enzymes for DNA replication

rationale: Poxvirus is the ONLY major DNA virus that replicates in the CYTOPLASM, not the nucleus. All other DNA viruses replicate in the nucleus. Because of this, Poxvirus must encode its OWN RNA polymerase (unlike other DNA viruses that use nuclear RNA polymerase). This also means the virus produces Guarnieri bodies (cytoplasmic inclusion bodies representing replication factories) visible on histology.

scenario: A 29-year-old man returning from the Democratic Republic of Congo presents with a 4-day history of fever, chills, and headache, followed by a vesicular rash starting on his face and spreading to his extremities. He also has swollen lymph nodes in his groin.
question: Which statement about the MANAGEMENT of this patient\'s likely condition is CORRECT?

options.A: Isolation is not needed since the rash has already appeared
options.B: Antiviral treatment with acyclovir is the first-line therapy
options.C: Supportive care is the mainstay; smallpox vaccination provides cross-protection; avoid direct skin-to-skin contact; report to public health authorities
options.D: The patient needs immediate liver biopsy to confirm the diagnosis

rationale: This presentation (travel from DRC + fever prodrome + centrifugal vesicular rash + LYMPHADENOPATHY) is classic for MONKEYPOX. Management includes: (1) SUPPORTIVE CARE (nutritious food, fluids, pain control); (2) Isolation and contact precautions; (3) SMALLPOX VACCINE provides cross-protection (JYNNEOS/ACAM2000); (4) Public health REPORTING; (5) Assess for secondary bacterial skin infections; (6) Severe cases may need antivirals (tecovirimat).

scenario: An epidemiology student is studying the global eradication of smallpox as a model public health achievement.
question: What is the CORRECT sequence of key smallpox eradication milestones?

options.A: Last US case 1977 → Last world case 1949 → WHO declared eradication 1980
options.B: Last US case 1949 → Last worldwide case Somalia 1977 → WHO declared smallpox eradicated May 1980
options.C: Jenner\'s vaccine 1896 → Last US case 1949 → WHO declaration 1976
options.D: WHO eradication 1970 → Last US case 1975 → Last worldwide case 1980

rationale: Correct smallpox eradication timeline: Edward Jenner demonstrated vaccination efficacy in 1796 → Last reported case in the US was in 1949 → Last case of smallpox worldwide was in SOMALIA in 1977 → WHO officially declared the world FREE OF SMALLPOX in MAY 1980 following a global intensive vaccination campaign. This remains the only human viral disease to have been eradicated.

scenario: A student is asked to differentiate between the Tanapox and Yaba monkey tumor poxvirus infections for a virology board exam.
question: Which statement CORRECTLY describes Yaba Monkey Tumor Poxvirus?

options.A: It causes localized skin lesions in Kenya and DRC; natural host is monkeys; mode of transmission is well-established
options.B: It causes benign HISTIOCYTOMAS that regress after about 5 weeks; can appear in lungs, heart, and skeletal muscles; natural human infections in Africa have NOT been observed
options.C: It causes malignant skin tumors in immunocompromised humans; classified in Orthopoxvirus genus
options.D: It cannot grow in monkey or human cell cultures, making laboratory diagnosis difficult

rationale: YABA MONKEY TUMOR POXVIRUS causes BENIGN HISTIOCYTOMAS (not malignant) that typically REGRESS after about 5 weeks. Multiple histiocytomas can appear in the lungs, heart, and skeletal muscles. Natural Yaba virus infections in HUMANS HAVE NOT BEEN OBSERVED IN AFRICA. Both Tanapox and Yaba monkey tumor viruses grow only in monkey and human cell cultures (with CPE) and are classified in the YATAPOXVIRUS genus.

scenario: A board exam question asks students to identify which poxvirus causes \'many benign skin nodules\' exclusively in humans.
question: Which poxvirus infects ONLY HUMANS and causes benign epidermal tumors (wart-like lesions)?

options.A: Monkeypox virus --- humans and primates
options.B: Orf virus --- sheep, goats, and humans
options.C: Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV) --- exclusively humans; causes benign epidermal tumors
options.D: Vaccinia virus --- humans and various animals

rationale: MOLLUSCUM CONTAGIOSUM VIRUS (MCV) infects ONLY HUMANS (obligate human pathogen) and causes BENIGN EPIDERMAL TUMORS --- the small umbilicated papules. This makes it unique among poxviruses. In contrast, monkeypox, cowpox, and orf are all zoonoses (infect animals AND humans). Smallpox (variola) also only infects humans but has been eradicated.

scenario: A student is reviewing the clinical classification of smallpox to prepare for board examinations.
question: Which subtype of Classic (Ordinary) smallpox shows CONFLUENT RASH on BOTH the face AND the extremities?

options.A: Discrete subtype --- distinct areas of normal skin between individual pustules
options.B: Semi-confluent subtype --- confluent rash on the FACE only, discrete elsewhere
options.C: Confluent subtype --- confluent rash on BOTH the face AND the extremities
options.D: Hemorrhagic subtype --- rapidly fatal with bleeding lesions

rationale: The Classic (Ordinary) smallpox has THREE subtypes: (1) DISCRETE --- distinct normal skin areas between individual, separated pustules; (2) SEMI-CONFLUENT --- confluent rash on the FACE but with discrete lesions elsewhere on the body; (3) CONFLUENT --- confluent rash on BOTH the face AND the extremities. The severity and prognosis worsen from discrete to confluent.

scenario: A student needs to understand what type of microscopy and special laboratory test are used to identify Tanapox vs. Orf virus from clinical specimens.
question: Which statement about the laboratory diagnosis of Parapoxviruses (Orf and Milker\'s Node) is CORRECT?

options.A: Cell culture using HeLa cells is the gold standard for parapoxvirus identification
options.B: Pap smear showing owl-eye inclusion cells is diagnostic for parapoxvirus
options.C: Electron microscopy can CONFIRM Parapoxvirus infection; but only PCR can DEFINITIVELY identify the specific parapoxvirus as Orf virus
options.D: Serology (IgM/IgG) is the primary diagnostic method for orf virus

rationale: Laboratory diagnosis of Parapoxvirus (Orf, Milker\'s Node): (1) ELECTRON MICROSCOPY can CONFIRM that the lesion contains a parapoxvirus (based on morphology), but cannot distinguish between orf, pseudocowpox, and other parapoxviruses; (2) ONLY PCR can DEFINITIVELY IDENTIFY and distinguish the specific parapoxvirus species as orf virus vs. pseudocowpox. Diagnosis is also made through direct examination and epidemiological history (contact with sheep). ----------------------------------------------------------------------- **TOPIC 4** *Hepadnaviridae: Hepatitis B & D Viruses* -----------------------------------------------------------------------

scenario: A 34-year-old man presents to the ER with jaundice, fatigue, and right upper quadrant pain. His ALT is 850 IU/L. He reports multiple sexual partners and no prior vaccination. Serology shows: HBsAg (+), anti-HBc IgM (+), HBeAg (+), anti-HBs (-).
question: What is the CORRECT interpretation of this serological profile?

options.A: Chronic HBV infection with low infectivity
options.B: Past HBV infection with resolved disease and immunity
options.C: ACUTE HBV infection with HIGH infectivity (HBsAg+, IgM anti-HBc+, HBeAg+)
options.D: Successful HBV vaccination response

rationale: This serological pattern is consistent with ACUTE HBV INFECTION WITH HIGH INFECTIVITY: HBsAg(+) = virus present; Anti-HBc IgM(+) = ACUTE infection (IgM is the marker that distinguishes acute from chronic); HBeAg(+) = HIGH INFECTIVITY and active viral replication; Anti-HBs(-) = no immunity yet. ALT elevation \>500 IU/L with jaundice confirms acute hepatitis B.

scenario: A 45-year-old nurse presents for routine HBV testing after completing her vaccination series. Her results show: HBsAg (-), anti-HBc (-), anti-HBs (+).
question: What does this serological pattern indicate?

options.A: Acute HBV infection in the window period
options.B: Chronic HBV carrier state
options.C: IMMUNITY FROM VACCINATION (anti-HBs positive WITHOUT anti-HBc)
options.D: Past natural infection with resolved immunity

rationale: Anti-HBs POSITIVE with Anti-HBc NEGATIVE indicates IMMUNITY FROM VACCINATION --- not from natural infection. Natural infection produces BOTH anti-HBs AND anti-HBc (total). Vaccination produces ONLY anti-HBs (no anti-HBc because there is no core antigen exposure). The recommended protective titer is ≥10 mIU/mL of anti-HBs.

scenario: A 28-year-old pregnant woman is found to be HBsAg-positive on routine prenatal screening. She is HBeAg-positive with high viral load.
question: What is the MOST APPROPRIATE management for her newborn immediately after delivery?

options.A: Administer 3 doses of HBV vaccine at 0, 1, and 6 months only
options.B: Give Hepatitis B specific immunoglobulin (HBIg) WITHIN 12 HOURS plus begin HBV vaccine series
options.C: No treatment needed if the mother is only HBsAg-positive without jaundice
options.D: Administer antiviral therapy (lamivudine) to the newborn immediately

rationale: Neonates born to HBsAg-positive mothers require DUAL PROPHYLAXIS: (1) Hepatitis B-specific immunoglobulin (HBIg) --- within 12 hours of birth (PASSIVE immunity); AND (2) HBV vaccine series --- providing ACTIVE immunity. HBeAg-positive mothers have HIGH infectivity and up to 90% vertical transmission risk. Babies born to anti-HBe-positive (not HBeAg-positive) mothers do NOT require HBIg but still need vaccination.

scenario: A 52-year-old man has had HBV infection for 8 years. His current labs show: HBsAg (+), HBeAg (+), elevated ALT, and HBV DNA 10⁷ copies/mL. He is being evaluated for antiviral therapy.
question: The risk for developing CHRONIC HBV infection depends on age at initial infection. Which statement about this relationship is CORRECT?

options.A: Adults have a 90% risk of developing chronic infection; infants have a 3% risk
options.B: The risk for chronic infection is DIRECTLY proportional to age --- older patients have higher risk
options.C: Approximately 90% of INFANTS develop chronic infection; only about 3% of ADULTS develop chronic infection
options.D: Risk is equal (50%) regardless of age at time of infection

rationale: The risk for CHRONIC HBV infection is INVERSELY PROPORTIONAL to age at infection: NEONATES/INFANTS: \~90% risk of chronic infection (immature immune system cannot clear the virus); CHILDREN (1--5 years): \~30% risk; ADULTS: only \~3% risk (mature immune system clears the virus in most cases). This is why perinatal vaccination is critical --- preventing chronic infection that leads to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.

scenario: A laboratory report comes back for a patient with suspected HBV infection. The ordering physician sees: HBsAg (+), anti-HBc IgM (-), anti-HBc total (+), HBeAg (-), anti-HBe (+), anti-HBs (-).
question: What is the CORRECT interpretation of this pattern?

options.A: Acute HBV infection with high infectivity
options.B: CHRONIC HBV infection with LOW infectivity (anti-HBe positive, anti-HBc total positive, no IgM)
options.C: Past HBV infection with full recovery and immunity
options.D: Window period of acute infection

rationale: This pattern = CHRONIC HBV INFECTION WITH LOW INFECTIVITY: HBsAg(+) = virus still present (\>6 months); Anti-HBc IgM(-) = NOT acute (IgM gone); Anti-HBc total(+) = confirms HBV exposure/infection; HBeAg(-) = seroconversion occurred; Anti-HBe(+) = LOW infectivity, end of active replication (usually); Anti-HBs(-) = not cleared yet. Anti-HBe is the marker of LOW infectivity vs. HBeAg which marks HIGH infectivity.

scenario: A physician is counseling a patient about the natural history of acute hepatitis B. The patient asks how long it typically takes to clear the virus and how often adults progress to chronic disease.
question: What percentage of IMMUNOCOMPETENT ADULTS clear HBV within 6 months of ACUTE infection?

options.A: 50% of adults clear the virus; 50% develop chronic infection
options.B: 95% of immunocompetent adults clear the virus within 6 months; \<5% develop chronic infection
options.C: Only 30% of adults clear the virus; 70% require antiviral treatment
options.D: 75% of adults clear the virus spontaneously; 25% require liver transplantation

rationale: 95% OF IMMUNOCOMPETENT ADULTS clear HBV within 6 months of acute infection --- this is the favorable natural history of HBV in adults. In contrast, chronic infection (defined as HBsAg persistence for \>6 months) develops in only about 3--5% of infected adults. The 5% who do NOT clear the virus are at risk for progressive liver disease, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.

scenario: A patient presents for evaluation of chronic HBV infection. His physician orders a panel of tests to assess infectivity, viral activity, and stage of disease.
question: What is the BEST INDICATION of ACTIVE VIRAL REPLICATION and HIGH INFECTIVITY in HBV infection?

options.A: Elevated serum bilirubin and jaundice
options.B: Presence of HBV DNA in serum (detected by PCR/molecular testing)
options.C: Positive anti-HBc IgG antibody
options.D: Elevated AST/ALT liver enzymes

rationale: HBV DNA in serum (detected by PCR or molecular testing) is the BEST INDICATOR of ACTIVE VIRAL REPLICATION and HIGH INFECTIVITY. It directly measures the amount of viral genetic material. Quantitative HBV DNA is also used to: (1) monitor response to antiviral treatment; (2) resolve questionable serologic results; (3) assess candidacy for therapy. HBeAg also indicates high infectivity but is a protein marker, not direct viral quantification.

scenario: A 62-year-old male immigrant from Southeast Asia is found to have HBsAg (+) for 12 years. He is now being evaluated for possible complications of chronic HBV.
question: Which is the MOST SERIOUS long-term complication of CHRONIC HBV infection?

options.A: Recurrent acute hepatitis flares requiring hospitalization
options.B: Renal failure from immune complex deposition
options.C: Progressive liver damage leading to cirrhosis and HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA
options.D: Secondary immunodeficiency with opportunistic infections

rationale: The MOST SERIOUS long-term complication of chronic HBV infection is PROGRESSIVE LIVER DAMAGE → Persistent hepatitis → Chronic hepatitis → CIRRHOSIS → HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA (HCC). Chronic HBV carriers have a 100-fold increased risk of HCC. HBV is classified as a Type I carcinogen. Regular monitoring with AFP and liver ultrasound is recommended for all chronic HBV carriers.

scenario: A healthcare worker is exposed to a needlestick injury from a patient known to be HBsAg-positive. She received HBV vaccination 3 years ago and her titer was documented as adequate (\>10 mIU/mL).
question: What is the APPROPRIATE post-exposure management for this previously vaccinated healthcare worker?

options.A: Immediately administer HBIg AND restart the full 3-dose vaccine series
options.B: No immediate action needed; check anti-HBs titer; if still adequate, no treatment required
options.C: Start antiviral therapy (tenofovir) for 28 days as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.D: Administer HBIg only; no vaccine needed since she was already vaccinated

rationale: For a PREVIOUSLY VACCINATED healthcare worker with documented adequate anti-HBs titer (≥10 mIU/mL): RECHECK anti-HBs titer; if still adequate → NO TREATMENT NEEDED (anti-HBs is protective). If titer is now inadequate → administer HBIg PLUS booster vaccine dose. HBIg alone (within 48 hours to 1 week of exposure) is for unvaccinated or non-responders. Due to HBV\'s long incubation period, the vaccine is also highly effective for post-exposure prophylaxis.

scenario: A patient is diagnosed with Hepatitis D virus (HDV) infection at the same time as a new HBV infection. Both infections were confirmed serologically.
question: What is this clinical scenario called, and how does it affect prognosis compared to HDV superinfection?

options.A: Co-infection --- simultaneous HBV+HDV; generally resolves like acute HBV; lower risk of chronic HDV than superinfection
options.B: Superinfection --- simultaneous HBV+HDV; higher risk of fulminant hepatitis and chronicity
options.C: Co-infection --- much worse prognosis than superinfection; almost always fatal
options.D: Superinfection --- occurs only in unvaccinated persons with no prior HBV exposure

rationale: CO-INFECTION = simultaneous acquisition of HBV + HDV. In co-infection, the patient usually recovers (like typical acute HBV), and chronic HDV develops in \<5% of cases. SUPERINFECTION = HDV infection occurring in a patient ALREADY chronically infected with HBV. Superinfection is MORE SEVERE --- causes worse acute illness, higher risk of FULMINANT HEPATITIS (5% fatality in acute phase), and almost always leads to chronic HDV infection with accelerated progression to cirrhosis.

scenario: A virologist explains to students why HDV (Hepatitis D virus) is classified as a \'defective virus.\'
question: Why is HDV (Hepatitis D virus) considered a DEFECTIVE VIRUS, and what specific component does it need from HBV?

options.A: HDV cannot replicate its RNA genome without HBV DNA polymerase
options.B: HDV requires HBV HBsAg (surface antigen) for its ENVELOPE in order to infect new cells
options.C: HDV lacks a nucleocapsid and needs HBV\'s core protein for structure
options.D: HDV cannot produce its delta antigen without HBV-encoded enzymes

rationale: HDV is a DEFECTIVE VIRUS because it cannot produce its own envelope proteins. HDV requires the HEPATITIS B SURFACE ANTIGEN (HBsAg) from HBV to form its ENVELOPE, which is necessary for virion assembly and infection of new cells. This is why HDV can ONLY infect individuals who are ALSO infected with HBV. HDV itself contains only a 1.7-kb single-stranded circular RNA genome and the delta antigen.

scenario: A medical student is studying the serological markers of HBV for a board exam. She must understand what each marker specifically represents in the timeline of infection.
question: Which HBV marker is described as \'the ONLY marker that can distinguish ACUTE from CHRONIC HBV infection\'?

options.A: Anti-HBs --- distinguishes vaccinated from infected individuals
options.B: HBsAg --- present in both acute and chronic infection
options.C: Anti-HBc IgM --- POSITIVE in acute infection; weakly positive in chronic; only marker distinguishing acute from chronic
options.D: HBeAg --- indicates high infectivity but present in both acute and chronic

rationale: ANTI-HBc IgM is the ONLY MARKER that can distinguish ACUTE from CHRONIC HBV infection: STRONGLY POSITIVE = consistent with ACUTE hepatitis B; WEAKLY POSITIVE = can be found in CHRONIC hepatitis B infection. IgM is the antibody class produced early in infection. Once infection becomes chronic, IgM levels decline and only anti-HBc IgG (total anti-HBc) remains. This makes IgM anti-HBc the critical diagnostic marker for acute HBV.

scenario: A physician is selecting antiviral therapy for a patient with chronic HBV infection who needs long-term treatment.
question: Which antivirals are used for CHRONIC HBV infection, and what is the main limitation of lamivudine?

options.A: Acyclovir and valacyclovir --- inhibit HBV DNA polymerase; well-tolerated with minimal resistance
options.B: Lamivudine, adefovir, tenofovir, and entecavir --- nucleoside/nucleotide analogues; RESISTANCE is a major problem, especially with LAMIVUDINE
options.C: Oseltamivir and zanamivir --- neuraminidase inhibitors effective against HBV replication
options.D: Ribavirin plus interferon --- only regimen effective against HBV; no resistance issues

rationale: Chronic HBV treatment uses NUCLEOSIDE/NUCLEOTIDE ANALOGUES: Lamivudine (first available, but HIGH RESISTANCE rate develops), Adefovir, Tenofovir (most potent, preferred), and Entecavir (second-line). These inhibit HBV\'s DNA polymerase/reverse transcriptase. RESISTANCE is the major limitation, especially with lamivudine. Liver transplantation is a last resort for liver failure. Interferon-alpha can also be used (alone or in combination).

scenario: A public health nurse is explaining HBV transmission to a community group. She lists the routes through which HBV can and cannot be transmitted.
question: Which body fluid is a major source of infectious HBV, and which fluid has NOT been shown to transmit infection despite containing HBsAg?

options.A: Blood is infectious; vaginal fluid and semen are NOT infectious
options.B: Blood (up to 10⁹ particles/mL) is the most infectious; tears, urine, and sweat contain HBsAg but have NOT been shown to transmit infection
options.C: Saliva is the primary route; breast milk is not infectious
